title: Curb Appeal: What Your Cafe Says Before Anyone Walks In
subtitle: Customers decide whether to enter in three seconds. The exterior is your first pitch.

toc.label: The A-Frame: Your Daily Street Pitch
Cleanliness and Maintenance as Brand Signals

metrics.label: a passerby decides to enter or walk past

sections.heading: The Three-Second Decision
sections.body:
A person walking past your cafe makes a decision in about three seconds: stop or keep walking. That decision is based entirely on what they see from the street.

They are not reading your menu. They are not checking your Google rating. They are registering a series of fast impressions:
- Does this look like a place I want to be?
- Is it open? Is it busy? Is it clean?
- Can I tell what it is and what it offers?
- Does it feel welcoming or closed off?

These are emotional, visual judgements made before any rational thought kicks in. And they are shaped by things most cafe owners stop noticing because they see the exterior every day.

The most dangerous condition is familiarity blindness. You walk past the faded sign, the cluttered window, the stained awning every morning and no longer see them. But every potential new customer sees them for the first time.

The fix is structured: audit your exterior regularly using fresh eyes, and treat it as a marketing asset, not just architecture.

sections.heading: Signage: Visible, Readable, Inviting
sections.body:
Your main sign has one job: tell people what you are and make them curious enough to approach.

**Visibility.** Can the sign be read from 20 metres away? Walk across the street and check. If trees, parked cars, or other signage obscure it, consider positioning, size, or a projecting sign that faces foot traffic.

**Readability.** Decorative fonts look beautiful on Instagram and are illegible on a fascia board. Choose a font that is clean and bold. Dark text on a light background, or light text on dark — high contrast always.

**Lighting.** If you trade in the dark months (most of the year in the Baltics), your sign needs illumination. An unlit sign after 4pm is an invisible sign. Warm-toned spotlights or backlit lettering work well.

**Information hierarchy.** The main sign shows your name. A secondary element — a tagline, a smaller sign, or window text — tells people what you do. "Specialty coffee & baked goods" is enough. Do not clutter with opening hours, phone numbers, or five different messages.

**Condition.** Peeling paint, faded colours, broken letters, uneven lighting — these signal neglect. If the sign looks tired, the customer assumes the coffee is tired too. Budget for sign maintenance or replacement every 3–5 years.

If your cafe is off the main path or on an upper floor, you need wayfinding: directional signs, pavement stickers, or an A-frame on the nearest busy street pointing the way.

sections.heading: Windows That Sell
sections.body:
Your windows are the most valuable display space you have. They are free, visible to every passerby, and communicate your cafe's personality without a word being spoken.

**What works:**
- A clear view inside. Customers want to see other people enjoying themselves — a busy, warm interior is the strongest invitation. Do not block windows with solid posters, menus taped to the glass, or tall shelving.
- Curated displays. A beautiful cake stand, a well-arranged coffee bag display, or seasonal decorations — visible from the street, changed regularly.
- Warm lighting. Interior light spilling through the windows creates an inviting glow, especially in dark or cold weather.

**What does not work:**
- Cluttered windows with overlapping posters, flyers, and notices. This signals chaos, not community.
- Dirty glass. Clean windows weekly — more often if you are on a busy road.
- Sun-faded displays. Anything in the window that faces direct sunlight fades within weeks. Rotate or replace.
- Too many stickers. Payment method stickers, TripAdvisor decals, delivery platform logos — each one individually is fine, but ten of them look like a taxi windscreen.

Use T145-02 (Window Display Planning Template) to plan monthly rotations. Seasonal themes work: spring flowers, summer iced drinks, autumn warmth, winter cosiness. Keep displays simple — one theme, three to five elements, clean background.

sections.heading: The A-Frame: Your Daily Street Pitch
sections.body:
The A-frame (pavement sign, sandwich board) is the single cheapest and most effective street-level marketing tool. It catches foot traffic that your fascia sign might miss, and it can change every day.

Rules for effective A-frames:

**One message.** Not three. Not a full menu. One thing that makes someone stop. "Fresh scones — out of the oven 10 minutes ago" is better than a list of ten items.

**Readable from 5 metres.** Large text, high contrast, clean handwriting or printed letters. If someone has to stop and squint, the message has failed.

**Personality.** The A-frame is where you can show character. A witty message, a hand-drawn illustration, a seasonal reference. This is not your fascia sign — it can be informal and fun.

**Change it daily.** A static A-frame becomes invisible. People who walk past every day stop noticing it. Change the message — even slightly — and it catches the eye again. Use T145-04 (A-Frame Daily Message Library) for a bank of ready-to-use messages.

**Positioning.** Place it where foot traffic flows, not pressed against your window. It should be in the pedestrian's sightline, angled toward approaching foot traffic, and never blocking the pavement (check local council rules on pavement signs).

**Condition.** A chalked A-frame in the rain looks terrible. Use a weatherproof board, or bring it in during heavy rain. Replace boards when they are dented, faded, or leaning.

Great A-frame messages:
- "Yes, we have oat milk."
- "You looked like you needed a coffee."
- "Today's soup: roasted tomato. Made this morning."
- "Rain outside. Warm in here."

sections.heading: Cleanliness and Maintenance as Brand Signals
sections.body:
A customer who sees a dirty exterior assumes the kitchen is dirty. It is not rational, but it is universal.

Exterior cleanliness and maintenance are brand signals. They communicate:
- We pay attention to detail
- We take pride in this place
- We are professional
- You can trust us

Build an exterior maintenance schedule (T145-03) that covers:

**Daily:**
- Sweep the entrance and pavement outside
- Clear any litter, cigarette butts, or leaves
- Wipe the door handle (yes, people notice)
- Check A-frame is positioned and legible

**Weekly:**
- Clean windows inside and out
- Wipe down exterior furniture (if applicable)
- Check all signage is intact and illuminated
- Water any plants or clean any displays

**Monthly:**
- Wash the fascia and awning
- Touch up any paint chips or scuffs
- Review and refresh window displays
- Check exterior lighting (bulbs, timers)

**Seasonally:**
- Deep clean the awning or canopy
- Repaint if needed
- Update seasonal decorations or displays
- Review signage for fading or damage

The owner or manager should do the "stranger walk" once a month: approach the cafe from the customer's perspective, walking up from both directions, and see it fresh. What looks tired? What is cluttered? What would you change if you were seeing it for the first time?

sections.heading: The Quarterly Exterior Audit
sections.body:
Use T145-01 (Exterior Audit Checklist) every quarter to formally assess your cafe's street presence. Score each area and track improvement.

| Area | What to Assess | Score (1–5) |
|------|---------------|------------|
| Main signage | Visible, clean, lit, readable | |
| Secondary signage | Clear, not cluttered, current | |
| Windows | Clean, uncluttered, inviting view inside | |
| Door and entrance | Clean, welcoming, accessible | |
| A-frame | Present, readable, positioned well | |
| Pavement and approach | Clean, free of litter, well-maintained | |
| Exterior furniture | Clean, stable, inviting (if applicable) | |
| Lighting | Working, warm, sufficient for dark hours | |
| Plants and decorations | Alive, clean, appropriate | |
| Overall impression | Would a stranger want to come in? | |

Score below 3 on any item = action needed this month.
Score below 2 = action needed this week.

Take photos during each audit. Comparing photos quarter-over-quarter reveals gradual deterioration you might not notice day-to-day.

Ask a friend or family member who does not visit regularly to do the audit with fresh eyes. Their observations will be more honest than yours.

The exterior is not a cost centre — it is a marketing channel. Every improvement you make here is seen by every person who walks past your cafe, every day. No paid advertisement has that reach.
